title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
This document presents a reproducible meta-analysis examining the effect of oral iron supplementation on haemoglobin (Hb) concentration in women of reproductive age (18–49 years). The analysis pipeline mirrors the approach I used in my published Cochrane abridged review on intravenous iron for non-anaemic iron deficiency (Dugan et al., 2022, J Cachexia Sarcopenia Muscle), adapted here for oral supplementation trials using a synthetic dataset for portfolio demonstration.
The full pipeline — from effect size calculation through to sensitivity analysis — is built in R using metafor and follows PRISMA 2020 reporting conventions.


Iron deficiency disproportionately affects women of reproductive age due to menstrual iron losses, pregnancy demands, and inadequate dietary intake. Oral iron supplementation is the frontline intervention, but the magnitude of Hb response varies considerably across trials — driven by differences in baseline iron status, dose, duration, and formulation.
A meta-analysis lets us pool effect sizes for a more precise overall estimate, quantify heterogeneity to understand why results vary, test for publication bias, and identify moderators (dose, baseline status) that explain variation in response.



The random-effects model assumes the true effect size varies across studies:
\[\hat{\theta}_i = \theta_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \theta_i \sim N(\mu, \tau^2), \quad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, v_i)\]
Where \(\hat{\theta}_i\) is the observed effect in study \(i\), \(\mu\) is the overall mean effect, \(\tau^2\) is between-study variance, and \(v_i\) is within-study sampling variance. This two-level structure is almost always appropriate for clinical nutrition research where populations, protocols, and contexts vary meaningfully.





Code
library(metafor)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

theme_set(
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "bottom"
    )
)

set.seed(2024)

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
2 Study-Level Data

2.1 Data Description
The dataset contains summary statistics from 18 RCTs examining oral iron supplementation versus placebo/control in women of reproductive age. Each row includes sample sizes, mean Hb change (g/dL), SDs, and study-level covariates (baseline iron status, dose, duration).






NoteData Source



The analytical pipeline demonstrated here mirrors the methods I applied in my published Cochrane abridged review on intravenous iron for non-anaemic iron deficiency (Dugan et al., 2022). For this portfolio demonstration, I’ve generated a synthetic dataset calibrated against effect sizes and variance structures from published oral iron RCTs (e.g., Low et al. 2016 Cochrane Review). The data are realistic but simulated — this page showcases the analytical workflow, not clinical findings.




Code
# Synthetic dataset calibrated against published oral iron RCTs
# Structure matches what I'd extract during systematic review screening

studies <- tibble(
  study_id     = 1:18,
  author       = c("Patterson 2005", "Chen 2008", "Khatib 2006",
                    "Rezk 2015", "Bencaiova 2012", "Makrides 2003",
                    "Cogswell 2003", "Falahi 2007", "Zhou 2009",
                    "Bruner 1996", "Verdon 2003", "Krayenbuehl 2011",
                    "Vaucher 2012", "Favrat 2014", "Pratt 2017",
                    "McClung 2009", "DellaValle 2014", "Pasricha 2013"),
  year         = c(2005, 2008, 2006, 2015, 2012, 2003,
                    2003, 2007, 2009, 1996, 2003, 2011,
                    2012, 2014, 2017, 2009, 2014, 2013),
  n_treat      = c(52, 78, 45, 60, 80, 215,
                    72, 34, 155, 36, 77, 90,
                    55, 110, 44, 88, 62, 200),
  mean_treat   = c(1.12, 0.85, 1.45, 0.92, 0.78, 0.65,
                    0.55, 1.30, 0.70, 0.42, 0.88, 0.95,
                    0.60, 0.72, 1.05, 0.68, 0.90, 0.58),
  sd_treat     = c(0.88, 0.72, 1.10, 0.65, 0.90, 0.82,
                    0.68, 0.95, 0.75, 0.55, 0.70, 0.80,
                    0.62, 0.78, 0.85, 0.72, 0.68, 0.70),
  n_ctrl       = c(50, 75, 43, 58, 82, 210,
                    70, 32, 150, 38, 75, 88,
                    53, 108, 42, 90, 60, 198),
  mean_ctrl    = c(0.15, 0.10, 0.18, 0.08, -0.05, 0.05,
                    0.12, 0.20, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.12,
                    0.08, -0.02, 0.15, 0.10, 0.05, 0.03),
  sd_ctrl      = c(0.82, 0.68, 1.05, 0.60, 0.85, 0.78,
                    0.65, 0.90, 0.72, 0.52, 0.65, 0.75,
                    0.58, 0.72, 0.80, 0.70, 0.65, 0.68),
  # Covariates for subgroup/meta-regression
  baseline_status = c("deficient", "replete", "deficient", "deficient",
                       "replete", "replete", "replete", "deficient",
                       "replete", "replete", "deficient", "deficient",
                       "replete", "replete", "deficient", "deficient",
                       "replete", "replete"),
  dose_mg      = c(100, 60, 120, 80, 65, 40,
                    30, 100, 60, 18, 80, 100,
                    40, 65, 105, 100, 80, 60),
  duration_wk  = c(12, 16, 8, 12, 16, 20,
                    12, 8, 16, 8, 12, 12,
                    16, 12, 8, 8, 12, 16)
)




2.2 Computing Effect Sizes
Using raw mean differences (MD) since all studies report Hb in g/dL — no need to standardise.
\[\text{MD}_i = \bar{X}_{T,i} - \bar{X}_{C,i}, \quad v_i = \frac{s^2_{T,i}}{n_{T,i}} + \frac{s^2_{C,i}}{n_{C,i}}\]


Code
# Raw MD — all studies use the same Hb units so standardisation isn't needed
dat <- escalc(
  measure = "MD",
  m1i  = mean_treat, sd1i = sd_treat, n1i = n_treat,
  m2i  = mean_ctrl,  sd2i = sd_ctrl,  n2i = n_ctrl,
  data = studies,
  slab = author
)

dat %>%
  select(author, year, n_treat, n_ctrl, yi, vi, baseline_status, dose_mg, duration_wk) %>%
  mutate(yi = round(yi, 3), vi = round(vi, 4), se = round(sqrt(vi), 3)) %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Study", "Year", "n (Tx)", "n (Ctrl)",
                   "MD (g/dL)", "Variance", "Baseline Status",
                   "Dose (mg)", "Duration (wk)", "SE"),
    caption = "Study-level effect sizes: Mean difference in haemoglobin change (g/dL)"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE, font_size = 12)

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
3 Random-Effects Meta-Analysis

3.1 Model Fitting
REML estimation for \(\tau^2\) — preferred over DerSimonian-Laird because it provides less biased variance estimates, particularly with <20 studies.


Code
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat, method = "REML")
summary(res)



Random-Effects Model (k = 18; tau^2 estimator: REML)

  logLik  deviance       AIC       BIC      AICc   
  2.7738   -5.5476   -1.5476    0.1188   -0.6905   

tau^2 (estimated amount of total heterogeneity): 0.0189 (SE = 0.0113)
tau (square root of estimated tau^2 value):      0.1376
I^2 (total heterogeneity / total variability):   60.06%
H^2 (total variability / sampling variability):  2.50

Test for Heterogeneity:
Q(df = 17) = 41.4706, p-val = 0.0008

Model Results:

estimate      se     zval    pval   ci.lb   ci.ub      
  0.7118  0.0434  16.3987  <.0001  0.6268  0.7969  *** 

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




3.2 Interpreting the Results


Code
pooled_md   <- round(coef(res), 2)
pooled_ci   <- round(res$ci.lb, 2)
pooled_ci_u <- round(res$ci.ub, 2)
tau2_val    <- round(res$tau2, 4)
I2_val      <- round(res$I2, 1)
Q_val       <- round(res$QE, 2)
Q_df        <- res$k - 1
Q_p         <- formatC(res$QEp, format = "e", digits = 2)


Key outputs:

Pooled MD = 0.71 g/dL (95% CI: 0.63 to 0.8)
\(\tau^2\) = 0.0189 — between-study variance beyond sampling error
\(I^2\) = 60.1% — proportion of variability due to genuine heterogeneity (>75% = high)
Cochran’s Q = 41.47 (df = 17, \(p\) = 7.99e-04)


3.2.1 Clinical Context
WHO defines anaemia in non-pregnant women as Hb < 12.0 g/dL. A pooled increase of ~0.71 g/dL is clinically meaningful — enough to shift a proportion of mildly anaemic women into the normal range. The wide prediction interval (explored below) tells a more nuanced story about who actually benefits.

text:
4 Forest Plot


Code
forest(
  res,
  header   = TRUE,
  xlim     = c(-2.5, 3.5),
  at       = seq(-0.5, 2.0, by = 0.5),
  xlab     = "Mean Difference in Haemoglobin (g/dL)",
  cex      = 0.85,
  col      = "steelblue4",
  border   = "steelblue4",
  mlab     = "RE Model (REML)",
  psize    = 1,
  fonts    = "mono",
  addpred  = TRUE,
  shade    = TRUE
)









The prediction interval (dashed) is wider than the confidence interval because it incorporates \(\tau^2\) — it shows where we’d expect a new study’s true effect to fall. If the prediction interval crosses zero, supplementation may not benefit all populations despite the pooled estimate being positive.

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
5 Heterogeneity Assessment

5.1 Quantifying Between-Study Variation
\(I^2\), \(\tau^2\), and Q give us numbers — the Baujat and GOSH plots show which studies are driving the heterogeneity.


Code
baujat(res,
       xlab = "Contribution to Overall Heterogeneity (Q)",
       ylab = "Influence on Pooled Result",
       main = "Baujat Diagnostic Plot")









Studies in the upper-right are both heterogeneous and influential — candidates for closer scrutiny (e.g., checking for non-standard dosing, open-label designs, or atypical populations).


5.2 GOSH Plot (Graphical Overview of Study Heterogeneity)


Code
# Fit model to all possible subsets — reveals whether heterogeneity
# is driven by specific studies (multimodal) or spread evenly (unimodal)
gosh_res <- gosh(res, subsets = 5000)
plot(gosh_res, out = 1, breaks = 50)









A unimodal cluster suggests quantitative heterogeneity (studies sampling from one distribution). Multiple clusters would indicate distinct subpopulations — which is exactly what we test next.

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
6 Subgroup Analysis

6.1 By Baseline Iron Status
The key biological moderator here: iron-deficient women have suppressed hepcidin, so they absorb a greater fraction of supplemental iron and show larger Hb responses. This is the mechanism I investigated extensively in the IRONWOMAN trial.
\[\text{Iron Absorption (\%)} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Hepcidin}}\]


Code
# Mixed-effects model with baseline_status as moderator
sub_status <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat,
                  mods = ~ baseline_status,
                  method = "REML")
summary(sub_status)



Mixed-Effects Model (k = 18; tau^2 estimator: REML)

  logLik  deviance       AIC       BIC      AICc   
  5.4498  -10.8996   -4.8996   -2.5819   -2.8996   

tau^2 (estimated amount of residual heterogeneity):     0.0105 (SE = 0.0084)
tau (square root of estimated tau^2 value):             0.1025
I^2 (residual heterogeneity / unaccounted variability): 45.38%
H^2 (unaccounted variability / sampling variability):   1.83
R^2 (amount of heterogeneity accounted for):            44.51%

Test for Residual Heterogeneity:
QE(df = 16) = 29.5785, p-val = 0.0203

Test of Moderators (coefficient 2):
QM(df = 1) = 7.7423, p-val = 0.0054

Model Results:

                        estimate      se     zval    pval    ci.lb    ci.ub 
intrcpt                   0.8430  0.0626  13.4700  <.0001   0.7204   0.9657 
baseline_statusreplete   -0.2162  0.0777  -2.7825  0.0054  -0.3685  -0.0639 
                            
intrcpt                 *** 
baseline_statusreplete   ** 

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
dat_def <- dat[dat$baseline_status == "deficient", ]
dat_rep <- dat[dat$baseline_status == "replete", ]

n_def <- nrow(dat_def)
n_rep <- nrow(dat_rep)

rows_def <- 3:(3 + n_def - 1)
rows_rep <- (max(rows_def) + 5):(max(rows_def) + 4 + n_rep)

res_def <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_def, method = "REML")
res_rep <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_rep, method = "REML")

par(font = 2)
forest(
  res,
  xlim     = c(-3.0, 4.0),
  at       = seq(-0.5, 2.0, by = 0.5),
  xlab     = "Mean Difference in Haemoglobin (g/dL)",
  cex      = 0.8,
  order    = baseline_status,
  rows     = c(rows_def, rows_rep),
  mlab     = "",
  header   = TRUE
)

par(font = 4)
text(-3.0, c(max(rows_rep) + 1, max(rows_def) + 3), pos = 4, cex = 0.85,
     c("Iron-Replete at Baseline", "Iron-Deficient at Baseline"))

addpoly(res_def, row = min(rows_def) - 1.5, mlab = "Subgroup RE (Deficient)", col = "firebrick")
addpoly(res_rep, row = min(rows_rep) - 1.5, mlab = "Subgroup RE (Replete)", col = "steelblue")









As expected from the hepcidin mechanism, iron-deficient women show a larger Hb response. This has direct implications for clinical practice: targeted supplementation (screen-then-treat) may be more efficient than universal supplementation, and the pooled effect in replete women may fall below clinically meaningful thresholds. This aligns with what we found in the IRONWOMAN population screening data, where baseline ferritin was the strongest predictor of treatment response.

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
7 Meta-Regression

7.1 Dose-Response Relationship
Meta-regression extends the RE model by including study-level covariates as predictors of effect size:
\[\theta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Dose}_i + u_i, \quad u_i \sim N(0, \tau^2_{\text{residual}})\]


Code
# Testing dose-response — biologically plausible up to ~100mg,
# beyond which mucosal block limits absorption
reg_dose <- rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ dose_mg, data = dat, method = "REML")
summary(reg_dose)



Mixed-Effects Model (k = 18; tau^2 estimator: REML)

  logLik  deviance       AIC       BIC      AICc   
 10.5066  -21.0131  -15.0131  -12.6954  -13.0131   

tau^2 (estimated amount of residual heterogeneity):     0.0019 (SE = 0.0048)
tau (square root of estimated tau^2 value):             0.0432
I^2 (residual heterogeneity / unaccounted variability): 12.94%
H^2 (unaccounted variability / sampling variability):   1.15
R^2 (amount of heterogeneity accounted for):            90.13%

Test for Residual Heterogeneity:
QE(df = 16) = 17.1927, p-val = 0.3732

Test of Moderators (coefficient 2):
QM(df = 1) = 22.1236, p-val < .0001

Model Results:

         estimate      se    zval    pval   ci.lb   ci.ub      
intrcpt    0.3249  0.0818  3.9704  <.0001  0.1645  0.4853  *** 
dose_mg    0.0055  0.0012  4.7036  <.0001  0.0032  0.0078  *** 

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
regplot(reg_dose,
        xlab   = "Daily Elemental Iron Dose (mg)",
        ylab   = "Mean Difference in Haemoglobin (g/dL)",
        main   = "Meta-Regression: Dose-Response Relationship",
        col    = "steelblue",
        bg     = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha = 0.3),
        plim   = c(10, 130),
        ci     = TRUE,
        pi     = TRUE,
        digits = 2,
        las    = 1)











7.2 Duration Effect


Code
# Longer supplementation should yield greater cumulative Hb increases,
# but plateaus as stores replete and hepcidin upregulates
reg_dur <- rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ duration_wk, data = dat, method = "REML")
summary(reg_dur)



Mixed-Effects Model (k = 18; tau^2 estimator: REML)

  logLik  deviance       AIC       BIC      AICc   
  2.5788   -5.1575    0.8425    3.1603    2.8425   

tau^2 (estimated amount of residual heterogeneity):     0.0206 (SE = 0.0124)
tau (square root of estimated tau^2 value):             0.1435
I^2 (residual heterogeneity / unaccounted variability): 61.47%
H^2 (unaccounted variability / sampling variability):   2.60
R^2 (amount of heterogeneity accounted for):            0.00%

Test for Residual Heterogeneity:
QE(df = 16) = 39.3846, p-val = 0.0010

Test of Moderators (coefficient 2):
QM(df = 1) = 1.1113, p-val = 0.2918

Model Results:

             estimate      se     zval    pval    ci.lb   ci.ub      
intrcpt        0.8887  0.1724   5.1563  <.0001   0.5509  1.2266  *** 
duration_wk   -0.0135  0.0128  -1.0542  0.2918  -0.0385  0.0116      

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




7.3 Multiple Moderators


Code
# Full model — which covariates independently predict effect size
# after mutual adjustment?
reg_full <- rma(yi, vi,
                mods = ~ dose_mg + duration_wk + baseline_status,
                data = dat,
                method = "REML")
summary(reg_full)



Mixed-Effects Model (k = 18; tau^2 estimator: REML)

  logLik  deviance       AIC       BIC      AICc   
  8.7413  -17.4826   -7.4826   -4.2873    0.0174   

tau^2 (estimated amount of residual heterogeneity):     0.0030 (SE = 0.0058)
tau (square root of estimated tau^2 value):             0.0549
I^2 (residual heterogeneity / unaccounted variability): 18.80%
H^2 (unaccounted variability / sampling variability):   1.23
R^2 (amount of heterogeneity accounted for):            84.11%

Test for Residual Heterogeneity:
QE(df = 14) = 15.8550, p-val = 0.3223

Test of Moderators (coefficients 2:4):
QM(df = 3) = 22.2214, p-val < .0001

Model Results:

                        estimate      se    zval    pval    ci.lb   ci.ub      
intrcpt                   0.1002  0.2256  0.4443  0.6568  -0.3420  0.5425      
dose_mg                   0.0068  0.0020  3.4016  0.0007   0.0029  0.0107  *** 
duration_wk               0.0082  0.0106  0.7724  0.4399  -0.0126  0.0289      
baseline_statusreplete    0.0452  0.1159  0.3897  0.6968  -0.1820  0.2723      

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1



7.3.1 Model Comparison


Code
# R^2 analogue: proportion of between-study variance explained
# R^2 = 1 - (tau^2_model / tau^2_null)

# Helper for AICc extraction — row naming varies across metafor versions
get_aicc <- function(model) {
  fs <- fitstats(model)
  idx <- grep("AICc", rownames(fs), ignore.case = TRUE)
  if (length(idx) > 0) return(as.numeric(fs[idx[1], 1]))
  return(as.numeric(fs[4, 1]))
}

tibble(
  Model = c("Null (no moderators)", "Dose only", "Duration only",
            "Dose + Duration + Status"),
  tau2 = round(c(res$tau2, reg_dose$tau2, reg_dur$tau2, reg_full$tau2), 4),
  R2   = round(c(0,
                  max(0, 1 - reg_dose$tau2 / res$tau2),
                  max(0, 1 - reg_dur$tau2 / res$tau2),
                  max(0, 1 - reg_full$tau2 / res$tau2)) * 100, 1),
  AICc = round(c(get_aicc(res),
                  get_aicc(reg_dose),
                  get_aicc(reg_dur),
                  get_aicc(reg_full)), 1)
) %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Model", "\u03C4\u00B2 (residual)", "R\u00B2 (%)", "AICc"),
    caption = "Model comparison: How much heterogeneity do moderators explain?"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

Note that \(R^2\) here represents proportion of between-study variance explained — not total variance. Unexplained heterogeneity likely reflects unmeasured factors like compliance, formulation type, dietary iron intake, and inflammation status.

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
8 Publication Bias

8.1 Funnel Plot


Code
funnel(res,
       xlab    = "Mean Difference (g/dL)",
       main    = "Funnel Plot: Assessing Publication Bias",
       col     = "steelblue",
       bg      = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha = 0.4),
       level   = c(90, 95, 99),
       shade   = c("white", "grey85", "grey75"),
       refline = coef(res),
       legend  = TRUE)









Asymmetry in the lower-left (missing small studies with null results) would suggest small-study effects. Points outside the 99% band are potential outliers worth investigating.


8.2 Egger’s Regression Test
Formalises the visual asymmetry assessment. A significant intercept (\(p < 0.10\) by convention) indicates funnel asymmetry — though this could reflect genuine small-study effects rather than publication bias specifically.
\[\frac{\hat{\theta}_i}{\text{SE}_i} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \frac{1}{\text{SE}_i} + \varepsilon_i\]


Code
egg <- regtest(res, model = "lm")
egg



Regression Test for Funnel Plot Asymmetry

Model:     weighted regression with multiplicative dispersion
Predictor: standard error

Test for Funnel Plot Asymmetry: t = 3.2850, df = 16, p = 0.0047
Limit Estimate (as sei -> 0):   b = 0.3207 (CI: 0.0798, 0.5616)




8.3 Trim-and-Fill Method
Estimates the number of “missing” studies and imputes them for an adjusted pooled estimate.


Code
tf <- trimfill(res)
summary(tf)



Estimated number of missing studies on the left side: 4 (SE = 2.8573)

Random-Effects Model (k = 22; tau^2 estimator: REML)

  logLik  deviance       AIC       BIC      AICc   
 -1.1809    2.3619    6.3619    8.4509    7.0285   

tau^2 (estimated amount of total heterogeneity): 0.0297 (SE = 0.0145)
tau (square root of estimated tau^2 value):      0.1724
I^2 (total heterogeneity / total variability):   67.40%
H^2 (total variability / sampling variability):  3.07

Test for Heterogeneity:
Q(df = 21) = 57.7473, p-val < .0001

Model Results:

estimate      se     zval    pval   ci.lb   ci.ub      
  0.6636  0.0469  14.1588  <.0001  0.5717  0.7555  *** 

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Code
funnel(tf,
       xlab    = "Mean Difference (g/dL)",
       main    = "Trim-and-Fill: Adjusted Funnel Plot",
       col     = "steelblue",
       bg      = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha = 0.4),
       refline = coef(tf),
       legend  = TRUE)









Trim-and-fill assumes asymmetry is entirely due to publication bias, which isn’t always true — small-study effects can arise from genuine biological reasons (e.g., smaller trials may select more deficient populations or use higher doses). I treat the adjusted estimate as a sensitivity check, not a corrected truth.

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
9 Sensitivity & Influence Analysis

9.1 Leave-One-Out Analysis
Refits the model \(k\) times, each time dropping one study. If removing a single study shifts the pooled estimate substantially, the result isn’t robust.


Code
loo <- leave1out(res)

par(mar = c(5, 10, 4, 2))
forest(
  x      = loo$estimate,
  ci.lb  = loo$ci.lb,
  ci.ub  = loo$ci.ub,
  slab   = dat$author,
  xlab   = "Pooled MD (g/dL) with Study Removed",
  main   = "Leave-One-Out Sensitivity Analysis",
  refline = coef(res),
  cex    = 0.8,
  header = "Study Removed"
)











9.2 Influence Diagnostics


Code
inf <- influence(res)
plot(inf, plotdfb = TRUE)
















Key thresholds: studentised residuals > |2| flag outliers; Cook’s distance identifies disproportionate influence; hat values > \(2p/k\) indicate high leverage. In my experience with iron supplementation meta-analyses, outlier studies usually trace back to methodological differences — different formulations, co-interventions, or population characteristics.

title: Iron Supplementation & Haemoglobin Response in Women of Reproductive Age
text:
10 Summary & Conclusions

10.1 Key Findings


Code
tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Pooled MD (g/dL)",
    "95% Confidence Interval",
    "95% Prediction Interval",
    "Between-study variance (tau^2)",
    "I-squared (%)",
    "Cochran's Q (p-value)",
    "Egger's test (p-value)",
    "Trim-and-fill adjusted MD",
    "Studies included (k)"
  ),
  Value = c(
    sprintf("%.2f", coef(res)),
    sprintf("%.2f to %.2f", res$ci.lb, res$ci.ub),
    sprintf("%.2f to %.2f", predict(res)$pi.lb, predict(res)$pi.ub),
    sprintf("%.4f", res$tau2),
    sprintf("%.1f%%", res$I2),
    sprintf("%.2f (p = %s)", res$QE, formatC(res$QEp, format = "e", digits = 2)),
    sprintf("p = %.3f", egg$pval),
    sprintf("%.2f", coef(tf)),
    as.character(res$k)
  )
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Summary of Meta-Analytic Results") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

  │  Study-Level Data (k = 18)  │
  │  Mean differences + SEs     │
  └──────────────┬──────────────┘

10.3 Reproducibility
This analysis is self-contained. To replicate:

Install required packages: install.packages(c("metafor", "tidyverse", "knitr", "kableExtra"))
Render: quarto render meta-analysis.qmd
All data, code, and outputs are embedded — no external files required

  Iron Biomarker Modelling — Body Iron Calculation Pipeline
  Implementation of the Cook et al. body iron formula using soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) and serum ferritin. Demonstrates the non-linear relationship between ferritin and total body iron stores — critical for identifying iron depletion in exercising females where ferritin alone is insufficient.

title: Projects
text:
Statistical Methods
The Cook et al. body iron formula linearises iron stores distribution for regression modelling:
\[\text{Body Iron (mg/kg)} = -\frac{\log_{10}(\text{sTfR} \times 1000\ /\ \text{Ferritin}) - 2.8229}{0.1207}\]
sTfR = soluble transferrin receptor (mg/L); Ferritin = serum ferritin (µg/L). Negative values indicate iron depletion.
This approach enables continuous body iron as a regression outcome rather than binary deficiency classification — particularly relevant when specifying linear mixed-effects models with repeated-measures iron status data.

text:
Education

Doctor of Philosophy — Nutrition & Exercise Physiology
The University of Western Australia | 2020–2024
PhD Title: “Iron and women’s health: A multidimensional exploration of iron deficiency, management, and treatment in active females”
This thesis provides a multidimensional understanding of iron-deficiency in women. Initial research systematically reviews contemporary literature, challenging diagnostic standards and evaluating treatment efficacy. Key insights were gained through targeted screening, revealing high prevalence and interplay between exercise, menstrual health, and iron metabolism among women. By integrating perspectives and preferences of women with empirical evidence, advancements were made in tailoring treatment approaches, underscoring the efficacy of intravenous-iron-therapy. Findings suggest a paradigm shift in iron-deficiency management for women.
Skills: Biostatistics, Clinical Trials, Epidemiology, Meta-Analysis, R Programming, Systematic Review

title: About
text: Researcher. Biostatistician. Builder.

title: About
text:
Background
I am a nutrition scientist and biostatistician with a PhD and over a decade of experience in clinical and translational research. My work sits at the intersection of women’s health, haematology, and applied statistics — with a growing focus on machine learning for health applications.
My research career began with a fundamental observation: iron deficiency in exercising females is chronically underdiagnosed, driven by male-calibrated reference ranges, symptom normalisation, and a near-total absence of large-scale intervention data. That observation shaped my doctoral work and has anchored my research ever since.

title: About
text:
Research & Clinical Experience
IRONWOMAN Randomised Controlled Trial — Lead researcher on a double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT examining iron supplementation in exercising females. Designed the protocol, managed randomisation, oversaw data collection across multiple timepoints, and led the biostatistical analysis.
Population Screening — Screened over 3,000 females for health indicators and markers of iron deficiency and anaemia across clinical and community settings, generating one of the largest observational datasets in this area.
ANZ Consensus Statement — Contributed to the Australian and New Zealand consensus position statement on iron supplementation in pregnancy, bridging the gap between clinical research and evidence-based practice guidelines.
Publications — Over 20 peer-reviewed articles in women’s health, nutrition science, clinical trials, and haematology.

title: About
text:
Statistical Expertise
My statistical toolkit spans the full range from classical inference to modern machine learning:

  Linear Mixed-Effects Models
  Longitudinal / Time-Series Analysis
  Meta-Analysis & Systematic Review
  Survival Modelling (Cox PH)
  ROC / Diagnostic Accuracy
  LASSO / Ridge / Elastic Net
  Bayesian Methods
  Power & Sample Size Calculation

Primary tools: R (tidyverse, ggplot2, lme4, survival, meta, pROC), Python (pandas, scikit-learn, PyTorch), SPSS, Stata.

title: About
text:
Current Transition: Research → Applied ML/AI
I am actively building toward applied data science and ML engineering roles in pharma, biotech, and health tech. Current upskilling:

Stanford AI in Healthcare Specialisation — Enrolled (2025–2026)
AIM-AHEAD Program — Python and PyTorch for health AI applications
R Formal Training — High Distinction, University of Western Australia
GitHub Portfolio — This site; showcasing reproducible, documented analyses

My target: roles that combine deep health domain knowledge with computational rigour — clinical data scientist, biostatistician (pharma/CRO), health AI/ML engineer, or research-to-product consulting.

title: Dr. Cory William Dugan
text:
About
My PhD and subsequent research have centred on iron deficiency in exercising females — a field defined by chronic underdiagnosis, male-calibrated reference ranges, and a near-total absence of large-scale intervention data.
I have screened over 3,000 females for health indicators and markers of iron deficiency and anaemia, led the IRONWOMAN double-blind randomised controlled trial, and contributed to the Australian and New Zealand consensus position statement on iron in pregnancy. To date I have published over 20 peer-reviewed articles on women’s health and related topics.
I am actively transitioning toward machine learning for health applications, combining formal R training (High Distinction, UWA), developing Python and PyTorch skills through the AIM-AHEAD program, and am currently enrolled in Stanford’s AI in Healthcare specialisation.
